title: Applications: bearings, elevation and gradients
summary: Navigators, surveyors and civil engineers all describe the world in three languages: direction, height and steepness. This lesson turns those languages into triangles you can solve. You will read three-figure bearings (measured clockwise from north, so due east is $090^\circ$ and $30^\circ$ is written $030^\circ$) and feed them into the sine and cosine rules to fix distances on a voyage; you will handle angles of elevation and depression and use their equality to move an angle from observer to object; and you will compute the gradient of an incline as a ratio, a percentage and an angle. Because Paper 2 is a calculation paper, every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards marks — the method sits on the page, mark by mark, so a slip in the final number does not cost you the working.
learningObjectives: Read and write three-figure bearings measured clockwise from north, and draw north lines to convert bearings into the interior angles of a triangle.
Apply the sine rule and cosine rule to bearing (navigation) problems once the interior angle has been found.
Solve problems involving angles of elevation and depression, using their equality to transfer an angle between two points.
Calculate and interpret the gradient of an incline as a ratio, a percentage and an angle of inclination via $m=\tan\theta$.

Model a real-world direction/height/steepness problem by drawing a triangle, choosing the correct trigonometric tool, and presenting working so method marks and follow-through protect the final answer.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: Maths for Navigators and Builders
simpleExplanation.summary: This topic uses trigonometry to find your way around and to measure how steep things are. The whole skill is turning a real-world problem of direction, height or slope into a triangle, then pointing the right trig tool at it.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture the compass at the top of a video game screen. A bearing is the exact heading it shows — 'go 072 degrees' — measured as a full turn clockwise from north. An angle of elevation is you tilting the camera up to spot a tower on the hill; an angle of depression is looking down from that tower to you. A gradient is how steep the ramp is that you must climb to reach it. Trigonometry is what converts those directions and heights into precise distances and angles.
steps: Sketch and label. Draw the situation, mark every known length and angle, and give the unknown a letter. For a journey, draw a fresh north line at every turning point.
Turn bearings into interior angles. Because all the north lines are parallel, alternate and co-interior angles convert the clockwise bearings into the angles inside your triangle.

Choose the tool. Right-angled triangle: SOH-CAH-TOA or Pythagoras. No right angle: sine rule if you have a side and its opposite angle, cosine rule if you have two sides and the included angle (or all three sides).
Solve and interpret. Keep full accuracy until the end, then round. Attach units, and write any bearing as a three-figure answer (for example $099^\circ$).

flashcards.back: A direction measured as an angle CLOCKWISE from north, always written with three figures. So $30^\circ$ is written $030^\circ$, due east is $090^\circ$, and $95^\circ$ is $095^\circ$.

flashcards.front: Turning two bearings into an interior angle
flashcards.back: Draw a north line at the turning point. The north lines are parallel, so alternate/co-interior angles convert the bearings into the interior angle of the triangle. A reliable route is: back-bearing of the incoming leg minus bearing of the outgoing leg (or the reverse), taken so the angle lies in $0^\circ$ to $180^\circ$.
The bearing of $A$ from $B$ when you know the bearing of $B$ from $A$: if the original is under $180^\circ$, add $180^\circ$; if it is over $180^\circ$, subtract $180^\circ$.

Elevation is measured UP from the horizontal to an object above you; depression is measured DOWN from the horizontal to an object below you. Both start from a horizontal line, never from the vertical.

The angle of depression from $A$ down to $B$ equals the angle of elevation from $B$ up to $A$ — they are alternate angles between two parallel horizontal lines. This lets you carry an angle from an observer to a distant point.
$m=\dfrac{\text{rise}}{\text{run}}=\dfrac{\text{vertical change}}{\text{horizontal change}}$. The run is the HORIZONTAL distance, not the slant distance along the slope.
Percentage gradient $=m\times 100\%$. A gradient of $0.08$ is an $8\%$ incline. It is still rise over run, just scaled by 100.
The gradient is the tangent of the angle of inclination: $m=\tan\theta$, so $\theta=\arctan(m)$. A $100\%$ gradient is therefore $45^\circ$, not vertical.
$\dfrac{a}{\sin A}=\dfrac{b}{\sin B}=\dfrac{c}{\sin C}$. Use it when you have a side and its opposite angle (a matching pair) plus one more fact. Keep the unknown on top.
$a^2=b^2+c^2-2bc\cos A$. Use it for two sides and the INCLUDED angle (SAS) — the exact shape most bearing problems take once the interior angle is found.
$\cos A=\dfrac{b^2+c^2-a^2}{2bc}$. Use it when all three sides are known (SSS); $\arccos$ returns the correct angle even when it is obtuse.
Bearings, elevation and gradient problems are all in DEGREES. A calculator left in radian mode makes every sine, cosine and tangent wrong. Check the mode before you start.

quickCheck.prompt: A direction of thirty degrees east of north should be written on your answer as a bearing how?
A ship sails from $A$ on a bearing of $050^\circ$ to $B$, then turns onto a bearing of $110^\circ$ to $C$. What is the interior angle $\angle ABC$ of triangle $ABC$?
From the top of a lighthouse the angle of depression to a boat is $18^\circ$. What is the angle of elevation from the boat up to the top of the lighthouse?
options: It cannot be found without the height

quickCheck.prompt: A road rises 60 m over a horizontal distance of 800 m. What is its gradient as a percentage?
A path has a gradient of $1$ (that is, $100\%$). What angle does it make with the horizontal?

faq.q: How do I turn two bearings into the angle I actually put in the sine or cosine rule?
faq.a: Draw a north line at the turning point. Because the north line there is parallel to the one at the start, the bearings become related by alternate and co-interior angles. A dependable recipe at a turn is: take the back-bearing of the leg you arrived on (original bearing $\pm 180^\circ$) and combine it with the bearing of the leg you leave on, choosing the difference that lands between $0^\circ$ and $180^\circ$. That difference is the interior angle of the triangle.

faq.q: How do I know whether to use the sine rule or the cosine rule?
faq.a: Look at what you have once the interior angle is found. If you have a side together with its opposite angle (a matching pair) plus one more fact, the sine rule works. If you have two sides and the angle between them (SAS), or all three sides (SSS), use the cosine rule. Most two-leg bearing problems become SAS — two distances with the included turning angle — so the cosine rule is the usual choice.

faq.q: Why do the angle of elevation and the angle of depression come out equal?
faq.a: Draw the horizontal line at the observer and the horizontal line at the object; they are parallel. The single line of sight cuts both, so the angle of depression at the top and the angle of elevation at the bottom are alternate (Z) angles between parallel lines, and alternate angles are equal. That equality lets you move a known angle from one end of the sight line to the other.

faq.q: Is a gradient the same thing as an angle?
faq.a: They describe the same steepness but in different units. The gradient is a ratio, rise over run, and can be written as a decimal or a percentage. The angle of inclination $\theta$ is linked to it by $m=\tan\theta$, so $\theta=\arctan(m)$. Do not confuse the two: a gradient of $1$ is a $45^\circ$ slope, and the slant distance along the slope is never the run — the run is horizontal.

faq.q: My calculator gives strange trig answers. What is the first thing to check?
faq.a: The mode. Every bearing, elevation and gradient question in this topic is in degrees, so the calculator must be in DEG, not RAD or GRA. A radian-mode setting silently corrupts every sine, cosine and tangent and is one of the most common ways students lose accuracy marks.

sections.content: From steering a ship across open water to designing a wheelchair-accessible ramp, trigonometry is the quiet partner behind navigation, surveying and construction. This lesson connects abstract triangles to those concrete jobs. We learn the three languages of the applied trig question — direction (bearings), height (elevation and depression) and steepness (gradient) — and use the sine rule, the cosine rule and SOH-CAH-TOA to answer them. As in every calculation topic, the worked examples are laid out the way our engine marks your own practice: the method is on the page, mark by mark.

A bearing describes the direction of one point from another. The convention used throughout the exam is to measure the angle from the north line in a clockwise direction, and to write it with three figures. So due east is $090^\circ$, south is $180^\circ$, and a direction thirty degrees east of north is written $030^\circ$, not $30^\circ$.

items: Measure from the north line (pointing vertically up the page), always clockwise.
Write every bearing with three figures: $30^\circ \to 030^\circ$, $95^\circ \to 095^\circ$.
For a multi-leg journey, draw a NEW north line at every turning point — the north lines are parallel.
Convert the bearings into the interior angle of the triangle using alternate and co-interior angles between those parallel north lines.

sections.content: The single most important step in a bearing problem is turning the given bearings into an interior angle of the triangle you can actually solve. Once you have that angle plus the two leg distances, the problem is an ordinary sine- or cosine-rule triangle.

sections.question:
A yacht sails from a port $P$ for 15 km on a bearing of $040^\circ$ to a point $A$. It then sails 25 km on a bearing of $130^\circ$ to a point $B$.
(a) Show that angle $PAB = 90^\circ$.
(b) Calculate the direct distance $PB$.
(c) Find the bearing of $B$ from $P$. [6]
sections.solution:
Sketch first: at $P$ draw a north line, measure $40^\circ$ clockwise, and draw $PA=15$ km. At $A$ draw a NEW north line parallel to the first, measure $130^\circ$ clockwise, and draw $AB=25$ km.

**(a) Interior angle at $A$.** The bearing of $A$ from $P$ is $040^\circ$, so the back-bearing of $P$ from $A$ is $040^\circ+180^\circ=220^\circ$. The bearing of $B$ from $A$ is $130^\circ$. The interior angle $PAB$ is the turn between these two directions at $A$:
$\angle PAB=220^\circ-130^\circ=90^\circ$.  [M1: back-bearing method] [A1: $90^\circ$]
So triangle $PAB$ is right-angled at $A$.

**(b) Distance $PB$.** With a right angle at $A$, use Pythagoras:
$PB^2=PA^2+AB^2=15^2+25^2=225+625=850$  [M1]
$PB=\sqrt{850}=29.2$ km (3 s.f.).  [A1]

**(c) Bearing of $B$ from $P$.** In right-angled triangle $PAB$, the angle at $P$ satisfies
$\tan(\angle APB)=\dfrac{AB}{PA}=\dfrac{25}{15}\;\Rightarrow\;\angle APB=\arctan\!\left(\tfrac{25}{15}\right)=59.0^\circ.$  [M1]
The bearing of $B$ from $P$ is the starting bearing plus this angle:
$040^\circ+59.0^\circ=099^\circ$ (to the nearest degree).  [A1]

**Final answer:** $\angle PAB=90^\circ$; $PB \approx 29.2$ km; bearing of $B$ from $P$ is $099^\circ$.

sections.content: These terms describe a line of sight relative to the horizontal. The angle of elevation is measured UP from the horizontal to an object above you; the angle of depression is measured DOWN from the horizontal to an object below you. A key fact: the angle of depression from an observer at $A$ down to an object at $B$ equals the angle of elevation from $B$ up to $A$, because the two horizontal lines are parallel and the sight line makes equal alternate (Z) angles with them. That equality lets you transfer an angle from one end of the sight line to the other.

sections.question: The angle of elevation from a boat to the top of a 90 m vertical cliff is $23^\circ$. The boat then sails directly away from the cliff until the angle of elevation is $15^\circ$. How far did the boat sail? [5]
sections.solution:
Let $T$ be the top of the cliff and $F$ the foot, with height $TF=90$ m. Let $A$ be the first boat position and $B$ the second. Both sight lines make right-angled triangles with the vertical cliff; the run in each is the horizontal distance from the foot.

**First position ($23^\circ$).**
$\tan 23^\circ=\dfrac{90}{FA}\;\Rightarrow\;FA=\dfrac{90}{\tan 23^\circ}=212.0\ldots$ m.  [M1][A1]

**Second position ($15^\circ$).**
$\tan 15^\circ=\dfrac{90}{FB}\;\Rightarrow\;FB=\dfrac{90}{\tan 15^\circ}=335.9\ldots$ m.  [M1]

**Distance sailed** is the difference of the two horizontal distances (carry full accuracy, round at the end):
$AB=FB-FA=335.9\ldots-212.0\ldots=123.8\ldots$ m.  [M1]
$AB \approx 124$ m (3 s.f.).  [A1]

**Final answer:** the boat sailed about $124$ m.

sections.content: The gradient measures the steepness of a slope. It is the ratio of the vertical change (the 'rise') to the horizontal change (the 'run'), and can be written as a fraction, a decimal or a percentage. Crucially, the run is the HORIZONTAL distance, not the slant distance travelled along the slope. The gradient is also linked to the angle of inclination $\theta$ — the angle the slope makes with the horizontal — by $m=\tan\theta$.

sections.question:
A straight mountain road climbs a vertical height of 84 m over a horizontal distance of 1.2 km.
(a) Find the gradient of the road as a percentage.
(b) Find the angle of inclination of the road.
(c) Find the actual distance travelled along the road surface. [6]
sections.solution:
First fix consistent units: $1.2$ km $=1200$ m, so rise $=84$ m and run $=1200$ m.

**(a) Gradient as a percentage.**
$m=\dfrac{\text{rise}}{\text{run}}=\dfrac{84}{1200}=0.07$  [M1]
$0.07\times 100\%=7\%$.  [A1]

**(b) Angle of inclination.** Using $m=\tan\theta$:
$\theta=\arctan(0.07)=4.00^\circ$ (3 s.f.).  [M1][A1]

**(c) Distance along the road** is the slant distance — the hypotenuse of the right-angled triangle with legs $84$ m and $1200$ m:
$d=\sqrt{84^2+1200^2}=\sqrt{7056+1440000}=1202.9\ldots$ m.  [M1]
$d \approx 1200$ m (3 s.f.), i.e. about $1.20$ km.  [A1]

**Final answer:** gradient $7\%$; inclination $\approx 4.00^\circ$; road distance $\approx 1.20$ km. Note how gentle a $7\%$ road is — a common error is to imagine a much steeper slope.

sections.content: Watch units in gradient problems. If a road rises 50 m over a horizontal distance of 2 km, convert both to the same unit first: $m=\dfrac{50}{2000}=0.025$, i.e. a $2.5\%$ incline. Forgetting to convert turns a gentle slope into an impossible cliff.

items: **Dropping the leading zeros on a bearing** — writing $30^\circ$ or $99^\circ$ instead of $030^\circ$ and $099^\circ$. A bearing is a three-figure answer; the missing figure can cost the final accuracy mark.

**Getting the interior angle wrong** — subtracting the two bearings blindly instead of drawing north lines and using the back-bearing. Take the difference that lands between $0^\circ$ and $180^\circ$.

**Confusing elevation with depression, or measuring from the vertical** — both angles are measured from the HORIZONTAL. Measuring from the vertical gives the complement and every length comes out wrong.
**Using the slant distance as the run** — the gradient and the angle of elevation both use the HORIZONTAL distance as the run, never the distance travelled along the slope.

**Confusing a gradient with an angle** — a $100\%$ gradient is a $45^\circ$ slope, not a vertical one. Convert with $\theta=\arctan(m)$, and do not report a gradient where an angle is asked (or vice versa).
**Mixing units** — combining metres and kilometres in a gradient, or degrees and radians in the calculator. Convert to one unit first and keep the calculator in DEG.
**Rounding too early** — quoting an intermediate distance to 3 s.f. and feeding the rounded value into the next part. Carry the full unrounded value and round only the final answer.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

On Paper 2 the marks are analytic: each is tied to a specific line of working — a method mark ($M$) for the correct approach, or an accuracy mark ($A$) for the right value, where an $A$ mark is DEPENDENT on the $M$ mark before it. Follow-through (FT) means a correct method applied to a wrong earlier number still earns the marks that depend on it, so one slip is punished once, not twice. But this protection only exists if the method is on the page. Study how each of the five marks below is earned by a specific line.

sections.question: A ship sails 12 km from port $P$ on a bearing of $040^\circ$, then 9 km on a bearing of $110^\circ$ to point $Q$. Find the distance $PQ$. [5]
sections.solution:
**Model answer — full working.**

Call the turning point $R$, so $PR=12$ km on bearing $040^\circ$ and $RQ=9$ km on bearing $110^\circ$.

**Find the interior angle at the turn ($R$).** Draw a north line at $R$. The back-bearing of $P$ from $R$ is $040^\circ+180^\circ=220^\circ$; the bearing of $Q$ from $R$ is $110^\circ$. A cleaner route to the same angle: at $R$, the ship turns from heading $040^\circ$ to heading $110^\circ$, an exterior turn of $110^\circ-40^\circ=70^\circ$, so the interior angle is
$\angle PRQ=180^\circ-(110^\circ-40^\circ)=180^\circ-70^\circ=110^\circ.$

**Now it is SAS:** sides $PR=12$ and $RQ=9$ with the included angle $R=110^\circ$, and we want $PQ$. Use the cosine rule.
$PQ^2=12^2+9^2-2(12)(9)\cos 110^\circ$
$PQ^2=144+81-216\cos 110^\circ=225-216(-0.342\ldots)=225+73.87\ldots=298.87\ldots$
$PQ=\sqrt{298.87\ldots}=17.29\ldots$
$PQ \approx 17.3$ km (3 s.f.).

**Final answer:** $PQ \approx 17.3$ km.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 5 marks:**

- **M1 — find the correct included angle from the bearings.** Awarded for the method $\angle PRQ=180^\circ-(110^\circ-40^\circ)$ (or the equivalent back-bearing $220^\circ-110^\circ$). This is a METHOD mark: it recognises that you have converted the bearings into the interior angle of the triangle, and it stands even if the arithmetic later slips.
- **A1 — the value $110^\circ$.** Awarded for stating $\angle PRQ=110^\circ$. This accuracy mark is dependent on the M1 above — you only earn it once the correct method has produced the correct angle.
- **M1 — cosine rule with substitution.** Awarded for writing $PQ^2=12^2+9^2-2(12)(9)\cos 110^\circ$ — the right rule with the right numbers in the right places. As a method mark it survives a later calculator slip.
- **A1 — the value $PQ \approx 17.3$ km.** Awarded for the correctly-rounded distance. The engine 'accepts answers that round correctly', so $17.3$ km scores; it does not insist on a fixed number of decimals, only a correctly-rounded value with its unit.
- **A1 (method / presentation) — the answer is supported by working.** The fifth mark rewards a complete, correctly-presented solution: the angle stated, the cosine rule shown, the substitution written, the value rounded with units. An unsupported '$17.3$' risks this mark.

**Follow-through (FT).** Suppose a student finds the interior angle wrongly as, say, $70^\circ$ (forgetting the $180^\circ-$ step). They lose the A1 for the angle, but if they then substitute THEIR $70^\circ$ correctly into the cosine rule, $PQ^2=12^2+9^2-2(12)(9)\cos 70^\circ$, they still earn the cosine-rule M1 and the FT accuracy mark for the distance that follows from their angle. A right method on a wrong number is penalised once, not twice — but only because the method is written down.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR working exactly this way — analytically, mark by mark, with follow-through. So always show the method: draw the north line, state the interior angle, name the rule, write the substitution, then the value with its unit and a three-figure bearing where one is asked. The method marks and follow-through are what protect your score when the final number slips, and you only get that protection if the working is on the page.
